ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese omelette curry with fried prawn. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) used as closest parent.

serving.description: 1 plate (curry with omelette and fried prawn, typically served over rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of omelette curry with fried prawn is approximately 450 g, comprising Japanese curry sauce with vegetables, a pan-fried egg omelette, and one or two pieces of deep-fried breaded prawn, typically served over steamed Japanese short-grain rice.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: Japanese curry with rice (USDA FoodData Central), egg omelette (USDA SR Legacy), and deep-fried breaded shrimp (USDA FoodData Central NDB 15152 / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Values estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% Japanese curry sauce with vegetables and rice, 30% egg omelette, 30% deep-fried breaded prawn. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for each component. Energy cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo analogous mixed curry-rice dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Japanese curry rice and analogous mixed rice dishes; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for omelette curry with fried prawn. GI of 52 estimated from Japanese curry rice (GI ~50–54, University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). The egg omelette and fried prawn components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate; the dominant glycemic driver is the curry sauce and rice base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (52 × 74.25) / 100 ≈ 38.6, where available carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 16.5 × 4.5 = 74.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry roux and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of this dish may contribute approximately 1890 mg sodium (420 mg per 100 g × 450 g), which approaches or exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt curry roux or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from curry sauce and rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low category) and a glycemic load of approximately 38.6 per full serving, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion with extra vegetables and monitor blood glucose response, as the fried prawn adds fat that may slow but not eliminate the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from egg omelette, fried prawn, and curry roux; high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of deep-frying oil, egg yolk, and Japanese curry roux contributes saturated fat (~9.5 g per serving) and cholesterol (~652 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider grilled prawn as a lower-fat alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Omelette Curry with Fried Prawn is a Japanese-style restaurant dish consisting of three main components: a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce (karē) with simmered vegetables such as onion, carrot, and potato; a soft pan-fried egg omelette placed on top of or alongside the curry; and one or two pieces of deep-fried breaded prawn (ebi furai). The dish is typically served over steamed Japanese short-grain rice and is a popular yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) comfort food. The curry sauce is made from a proprietary Japanese curry roux block, which gives it a smooth, thick consistency and a mild, slightly sweet spice profile distinct from South Asian curries. The omelette adds richness and a creamy egg flavour, while the fried prawn provides a crispy textural contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~756 kcal per 450 g serving), moderate in protein (~41 g), and relatively high in sodium (~1890 mg) due to the curry roux. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is substantial (~38.6) owing to the rice and curry sauce carbohydrates. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension due to its sodium content, and warrants portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It contains common allergens including eggs, wheat (curry roux and breadcrumbs), and crustaceans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Japanese curry rice, egg omelette, and deep-fried breaded shrimp. GI was estimated from the closest analogous food (Japanese curry rice) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. All other -1 fields are not applicable or not reported in the component sources. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite dish as served (curry sauce, omelette, fried prawn, and rice combined). The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as this is a composite dish containing curry, egg, seafood, and rice. The input cuisine tag 'Japanese Curry' is preserved in aliases. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese curry restaurant portions in Singapore. GL per serving = (52 × 74.25) / 100 = 38.6, where carbohydrate per serving = 16.5 g/100g × 450 g = 74.25 g.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Dairy and eggs' (input) to 'Mixed' as this is a composite dish.

Estimated nutrient values via weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy approved sources.

Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency per Q8 aliases consistency rule.
Set gi_category = 'low' for GI 52 per strict ≤55 threshold rule.
Calculated GL per serving = (52 × 74.25) / 100 = 38.6 and documented calculation in gi_source notes.
